does: This paper introduces CEO-Bench, a multi-agent simulation benchmark designed to evaluate large language models on high-stakes strategic decision-making tasks. The benchmark places LLM agents in the role of a CEO who must allocate capital across business units over multiple rounds while receiving conflicting advice from role-conditioned C-suite advisors (CFO, CTO, COO, CMO), each possessing private information and distinct organizational priorities. The LLM must integrate these diverse perspectives under information asymmetry and operational constraints, then produce concrete allocation plans. Performance is evaluated across four dimensions: role integration (effectively synthesizing advisor input), conditional boldness (appropriate risk-taking given context), history-sensitive judgment (learning from prior outcomes), and plan validity (respecting organizational constraints). This setup moves beyond stylized reasoning tasks to assess how LLMs handle the realistic complexity of executive decision-making.
connects: This work builds on growing efforts to evaluate LLM decision-making capabilities beyond isolated cognitive benchmarks like reasoning and knowledge retrieval. It extends prior work on economic rationality evaluation and game-theoretic reasoning by grounding assessment in concrete organizational simulation. The multi-agent framework connects to research on agent-based modeling and multi-stakeholder collaboration, while the emphasis on conflicting recommendations and information asymmetry draws from organizational economics and principal-agent theory. The benchmark responds to critiques that existing LLM evaluations overlook real-world constraints: temporal dependencies, organizational hierarchies, and the need to synthesize rather than retrieve correct answers. It combines insights from behavioral economics, organizational behavior, and multi-agent AI systems to create a more ecologically valid testing ground for LLM capabilities.
enables: This benchmark enables future work on evaluating and improving LLM performance on complex, realistic organizational tasks that require genuine strategic synthesis rather than isolated reasoning. It opens directions for studying how LLMs learn from organizational feedback over time, handle stakeholder management and persuasion, and adapt to changing constraints. The framework could support research into interpretability of strategic reasoning—understanding which advice LLMs weight most heavily and why. Beyond evaluation, CEO-Bench could drive development of better prompting strategies, fine-tuning approaches, and architectural improvements for multi-perspective decision-making. The benchmark also enables comparative studies across different LLM architectures and sizes on realistic tasks. Finally, it establishes methodology for creating domain-specific multi-agent simulations to evaluate AI systems on high-stakes professional decisions, potentially extending to other executive roles, public policy contexts, or complex coordination scenarios.
